name: Stripe
tagline: Developer-first payment infrastructure that lets businesses accept, manage, and optimize online transactions globally
description: Stripe is a payment processing platform that provides the technical infrastructure for internet commerce. Instead of building payment systems from scratch, businesses integrate Stripe's APIs to accept payments, manage subscriptions, handle payouts, and prevent fraud across 135+ currencies and 40+ countries. Stripe's differentiating advantage is its developer experience — its API-first design, comprehensive documentation, and client libraries in 10+ languages make it the default choice for technology companies. The platform processes hundreds of billions of dollars annually and serves businesses from one-person startups to publicly traded enterprises.
pros: API-first design with client libraries in Python, Ruby, Node.js, PHP, Java, Go, .NET, and Swift, enabling full payment workflow integration in hours rather than weeks compared to traditional processors
Unified API handles 135+ currencies, 40+ payment methods (credit cards, Apple Pay, Google Pay, SEPA, iDEAL, Alipay, WeChat Pay), and local acquiring in 40+ countries from a single integration
Stripe Radar uses machine learning trained on Stripe's network-wide transaction data to block fraud in real-time with 99.9% availability and configurable rules that require no ML expertise to tune

Subscription management with Stripe Billing automates proration, dunning, invoicing, and Smart Retries that recover 30-50% of failed recurring payments by retrying at optimal times based on card network data

Stripe Connect enables marketplace and platform businesses to onboard sub-merchants, split payments, handle KYC/identity verification, and manage payouts to thousands of sellers with a single integration
cons: Standard transaction fee of 2.9% + $0.30 per card charge is premium pricing compared to PayPal's 2.59% or Adyen's interchange-plus model, with no volume discounts until processing exceeds $1 million annually

Account holds and reserve requirements can be imposed without warning on high-risk businesses (crowdfunding, travel bookings, subscription boxes), freezing 10-25% of funds for 90-180 days with limited appeals process

Customer support for standard accounts is email-only with 24-48 hour response times and no phone support for accounts processing under $1 million per month, creating frustration during time-sensitive payment disputes

features.name: Payments API
features.description: Accept credit/debit cards, digital wallets, and local payment methods through a single API with 40+ method support

features.name: Subscriptions & Billing
features.description: Recurring billing with proration, metered billing, coupons, dunning management, and Smart Retries for failed payments

features.name: Connect Platform
features.description: Marketplace payment infrastructure with sub-merchant onboarding, payment splitting, and instant payouts via Stripe Treasury

features.name: Radar Fraud Prevention
features.description: ML-based fraud detection with customizable rules, 3D Secure authentication, and dispute management automation

features.name: Invoicing
features.description: Create and send invoices with automatic collection, payment reminders, and multiple payment method support

features.name: Payment Links
features.description: No-code payment page generator for businesses that want to accept payments without API integration

features.name: Checkout
features.description: Pre-built, customizable payment page with Apple Pay, Google Pay, and Link autofill for optimized conversion

features.name: Terminal
features.description: In-person payment processing with Stripe Reader hardware and SDK for POS and mobile payment applications

features.name: Tax
features.description: Automated sales tax calculation and collection across US states, EU VAT, and international tax jurisdictions

features.name: Identity
features.description: AI-powered identity verification for KYC compliance, age verification, and fraud prevention use cases

features.name: Atlas
features.description: Company formation, US bank account, and tax registration for international founders incorporating in Delaware

features.name: Treasury
features.description: Banking-as-a-service API for holding funds, making transfers, and issuing cards through Stripe's banking partners

features.name: Climate
features.description: Carbon removal contribution as a percentage of transaction volume, integrated directly into the payments dashboard

features.name: Data Pipeline
features.description: Automated data export to data warehouses (Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift) for custom analytics and reconciliation

features.name: Revenue & Finance Automation
features.description: Automated revenue recognition, journal entries, and reconciliation for subscription businesses with GAAP/IFRS compliance

features.name: Sigma
features.description: SQL-based custom query interface for payment data with pre-built reports for MRR, churn, refund rates, and dispute metrics

features.name: API Keys & Webhooks
features.description: Granular API key permissions, webhook signatures for event verification, and idempotency guarantees for safe retries

features.name: Stripe Apps
features.description: Third-party app marketplace for extending the Stripe dashboard with integrations from QuickBooks, NetSuite, Xero, and Salesforce

content.title: Executive Summary
content.body: Stripe is the dominant payment infrastructure platform for internet businesses, processing hundreds of billions of dollars in annual transaction volume across 135+ currencies and 40+ countries. Its API-first architecture set the industry standard for developer experience — a payments integration that traditionally took weeks with legacy processors can be deployed in hours using Stripe's client libraries in Python, Ruby, Node.js, PHP, Java, Go, .NET, and Swift. The platform has expanded far beyond payment acceptance into a comprehensive financial services stack: Stripe Billing for subscription management and Smart Retries that recover 30-50% of failed recurring payments, Stripe Connect for marketplace payment splitting with automated KYC verification, Stripe Tax for multi-jurisdiction tax calculation across 12,000+ tax regimes, Stripe Treasury for embedded banking services, and Stripe Atlas for company formation. Stripe operates on a transparent per-transaction pricing model (2.9% + $0.30 for standard card charges) with no monthly fees, setup costs, or minimum processing requirements, which makes it accessible to startups while serving publicly traded enterprises. The company is privately valued at $50+ billion and serves millions of businesses from one-person shops to Fortune 500 enterprises.

content.title: Best For
content.body: Stripe is the optimal payment processor for three business categories. SaaS and subscription businesses benefit from Stripe Billing's automated dunning — emails sent on a configurable schedule after failed payment attempts — combined with Smart Retries that analyze card network response codes to determine optimal retry timing, recovering 30-50% of recurring revenue that would otherwise be lost to involuntary churn. E-commerce businesses selling internationally gain instant access to 40+ local payment methods (iDEAL in Netherlands, Bancontact in Belgium, Alipay in China, SEPA in EU) through a single integration, eliminating the need for separate local payment processor relationships per market. Marketplace and platform businesses building two-sided networks use Stripe Connect to onboard sellers across 40+ countries with automated Know Your Customer (KYC) verification, split payments between platform and seller in real time, hold funds in escrow, and facilitate instant payouts via debit cards — capabilities that would require 6-12 months of development effort to build internally. Startups in their first 12 months particularly benefit from Stripe's instant account activation, transparent pricing without volume minimums, and the ability to accept payments with a single API call.

content.title: Worst Fit
content.body: Stripe is a poor fit for four scenarios. Businesses in high-risk categories — CBD, vaping products, adult content, firearms and ammunition, debt collection, credit repair, and certain financial services — face frequent account freezes, reserve requirements of 10-25% of funds held for 90-180 days, and permanent account termination with limited appeals process. These businesses should seek specialized high-risk processors that understand their industry's regulatory landscape and underwriting requirements. Non-technical founders and small businesses without development resources will find Stripe's API-first approach intimidating — while Stripe offers Payment Links and Checkout as no-code options, these are limited in customization, and any change beyond basic payment acceptance requires developer involvement. Organizations processing over $5 million annually should negotiate custom pricing with Stripe or evaluate interchange-plus processors like Adyen that pass through card network fees at cost, which typically results in 15-30% lower effective rates at that volume. Businesses that primarily accept in-person payments — retail stores, restaurants, service providers — will find Square's integrated hardware-software ecosystem more natural, with card readers that pair instantly, point-of-sale software built for specific verticals, and same-day settlement that Stripe Terminal does not match in ease of use.

content.title: Key Features
content.body: Stripe's payment infrastructure is the foundation, but its moat is the layer above: Stripe Billing handles subscription lifecycles with proration and dunning, Stripe Connect manages marketplace payouts with KYC compliance, and Stripe Radar blocks fraud across millions of merchants.
items: Stripe Billing provides a complete subscription management system including plan configuration, trial management, prorated upgrades/downgrades, metered billing, coupon application, dunning email automation, and Smart Retries that analyze card network decline codes to retry at optimal times.

Stripe Connect enables marketplace platforms to onboard sub-merchants with custom KYC/identity verification, facilitate complex payment routing with marketplace fees, hold funds in escrow accounts, and offer instant payouts through Stripe Treasury-issued debit cards.

Radar for Fraud Prevention scores every transaction in under 100ms using machine learning models trained on Stripe's global transaction network, with customizable rule sets, 3D Secure 2 authentication with liability shift, and automated dispute response.

Stripe Tax automatically determines applicable sales tax rates across 12,000+ US state, county, city, and EU VAT jurisdictions based on product tax codes and customer location, applies them to transactions, and generates jurisdiction-level reports for filing.

Sigma provides direct SQL query access to Stripe account data, enabling custom analytics for metrics like monthly recurring revenue (MRR), customer lifetime value (LTV) cohorts, refund rate trends by product, and dispute win/loss analysis without data extraction.

content.title: Real Advantages
content.body: Stripe's strongest differentiator is its API design quality and developer experience. The documentation is widely considered the best in financial services — every endpoint has clear examples in multiple languages, a test mode with realistic test card numbers that simulate every failure scenario (insufficient funds, expired card, lost card, stolen card, regulatory decline), and a dashboard that shows live API request logs with response times and error details. This developer experience translates directly to faster time-to-market: a payments integration that requires 2-4 weeks with traditional processors can be production-ready in 2-3 days with Stripe. The network effects of Radar are another genuine moat — because Stripe processes transactions across millions of businesses, Radar's fraud detection models learn from a broader data set than any individual merchant's transaction history, detecting patterns that a standalone fraud system would miss. Stripe's expansion into adjacent financial services (Treasury, Tax, Identity, Capital, Climate) creates increasing switching costs: a business using Stripe for payments, Tax for compliance, Treasury for banking, and Capital for financing would face prohibitive migration complexity, making Stripe progressively more embedded in its operations over time.

content.title: Real Limitations
content.body: Customer support is Stripe's most persistent weakness. Standard accounts processing under $1 million per month receive email-only support with 24-48 hour response times, no phone support, and no dedicated account management. During payment disputes, chargebacks, or account reserve notifications — situations where timely communication directly impacts cash flow — slow support responses create genuine financial risk for businesses. The 2.9% + $0.30 standard transaction fee is premium pricing compared to interchange-plus processors that charge a markup over the actual card network fee (typically 0.2-0.5% + $0.10 markup). For a business processing $500,000 annually with an average ticket of $50, this translates to roughly $3,000 more per year with Stripe versus an interchange-plus processor. Account stability is another concern: Stripe can place holds or reserves on accounts without prior warning, particularly for businesses in industries Stripe deems higher risk, and the appeals process is opaque with limited escalation options. Stripe's Terms of Service allow termination with 30 days notice for any reason, creating a concentration risk for businesses that become dependent on Stripe's infrastructure.

content.title: Pricing Explained
content.body: Stripe uses transparent per-transaction pricing with no monthly fees, setup costs, or minimum processing requirements. Standard card charges cost 2.9% + $0.30 per successful transaction. International cards incur an additional 1.5% fee. ACH direct debit is 0.8% capped at $5. Instant card payouts cost 1% (minimum $0.50). Stripe Billing adds 0.5% of recurring revenue for subscription management. Radar fraud protection costs $0.02 per transaction for the standard tier and $0.05 for the advanced tier with custom machine learning models. Stripe Connect for marketplaces charges 0.25% + $0.25 per transaction for payment routing plus account fees. Stripe Tax costs 0.4% of the transaction amount for automatic tax calculation. A critical pricing nuance: Stripe does not offer volume discounts until processing exceeds $1 million annually, and even then discounts are negotiated individually rather than automatically applied. Businesses processing consistent cross-border transactions should note that the 1.5% international card fee plus standard processing means a cross-border sale can cost 4.4% + $0.30, significantly eroding margins on high-volume, low-margin international sales.

content.title: Hidden Costs
content.body: Three hidden costs surprise Stripe adopters. Dispute fees are the most common: each chargeback costs $15 in addition to the original transaction fee being reversed, and Stripe does not refund the dispute fee even if the merchant wins the dispute. For businesses with 0.5-1% dispute rates, this adds $0.08-0.15 per successful transaction in hidden cost — effectively raising the transaction cost by 5-10% above the advertised 2.9% rate. Currency conversion costs for Stripe's automatic settlement in accounts holding a different currency than the transaction currency incur a 2% markup on the wholesale exchange rate, which is significantly worse than dedicated currency exchange services or multi-currency accounts. The third hidden cost is the premium for additional Stripe products: using Stripe Billing, Stripe Tax, Radar, and Connect simultaneously can add 1-2% to effective transaction costs. A business using all four products on a $50 transaction pays approximately $2.15 (standard fee) + $0.25 (Billing at 0.5%) + $0.20 (Tax at 0.4%) + $0.02 (Radar) = $2.62, or 5.24% — nearly double the base rate. Platform lock-in costs are harder to quantify but real: Stripe's custom data models and API patterns require significant re-engineering to migrate away.

content.title: Learning Curve
content.body: Basic payment integration — creating a Checkout Session, handling the success/cancel redirect, listening to webhook events for payment confirmation — takes 2-4 hours for developers with experience in any server-side language and basic understanding of REST APIs. The developer documentation, code examples in 8+ languages, and pre-built UI components (Stripe Elements, Checkout, Payment Element) significantly reduce the learning curve. Intermediate proficiency — configuring subscription products with trial periods and proration, setting up webhook endpoints with signature verification and idempotency for at-least-once delivery, handling SCA authentication flows, and implementing multi-currency pricing — requires 1-2 weeks of development and testing. Advanced integration — building a Connect platform with custom onboarding flows, configuring Tax with product tax codes and customer exemptions, implementing Sigma reports for finance teams, and designing Smart Retry configurations optimized for specific card network behavior — demands 4-8 weeks for developers unfamiliar with payments domain concepts like settlement timing, network fees, and regulatory requirements. Non-developers can use Stripe's no-code options (Payment Links, Invoice, Checkout) with a learning curve of 1-2 hours.

content.title: Setup Time
content.body: A basic Stripe account accepting one-time payments via Checkout can be operational in 2-4 hours: create an account (10 minutes, subject to verification which can take 2-24 hours depending on business type and documentation), integrate Stripe Checkout using the pre-built payment page (1-2 hours for a developer), configure webhook endpoints (30 minutes), test with test mode card numbers (30 minutes), and deploy to production (30 minutes). A subscription billing setup with Stripe Billing requires 1-3 days: configure product catalog and pricing models, implement proration logic for plan changes, set up dunning email sequences for failed payments, test webhook handling for subscription lifecycle events (trial_starting, customer.subscription.updated, invoice.payment_succeeded, invoice.payment_failed), and configure Smart Retry rules. A full Stripe Connect marketplace implementation requires 2-4 weeks: design the onboarding flow (standard vs. express vs. custom accounts), implement KYC verification collection, build payment splitting logic, test with multiple seller scenarios in test mode, and configure payout scheduling. Tax configuration requires 2-5 days for correct product tax code mapping and exemption certificate management across all jurisdictions where customers are located.

content.title: Migration Difficulty
content.body: Migrating from a legacy payment processor to Stripe is moderately difficult, rated 6/10 for most businesses. The primary challenge is the dual-running period: the existing processor must remain active while Stripe integration is built and tested, then the transition requires cutting over customers from old saved card data to Stripe's payment methods. Stripe's Migration API and Customer Portal help with this transition, but customers with saved credit cards on the legacy processor must re-enter their payment information or migrate through a card-updater service (charge typically $0.10-0.25 per successful update). Subscription migration is the most complex scenario: active subscriptions with upcoming renewal dates, various billing frequencies, and different trial end dates must be mapped to Stripe's subscription data model, and the timing of the cutover must be coordinated to avoid double-charging or missing a billing cycle. Stripe provides a data migration service for high-volume accounts that handles data mapping and validation, but it requires 2-4 weeks of planning and testing. Migration from Square is simpler (4/10 difficulty) due to similar data models, while migration from legacy processors like First Data or Worldpay is more complex (8/10) due to custom data formats and proprietary tokenization schemes.

content.title: Integration Ecosystem
content.body: Stripe integrates with 200+ third-party applications through its Apps Marketplace and direct partnerships. The most impactful integrations span e-commerce platforms (Shopify, WooCommerce, BigCommerce, Magento — native plugins that configure Stripe as a payment gateway with minimal setup), accounting software (QuickBooks, Xero, NetSuite, FreshBooks — automated transaction sync that eliminates manual reconciliation, with Stripe Smart Reports mapping payment data to accounting categories), CRM tools (Salesforce, HubSpot — payment and subscription data synchronization for customer 360 views), analytics platforms (Mixpanel, Amplitude — behavioral event export for conversion funnel analysis), and development tools (GitHub, Vercel, Netlify, VSCode extensions — deployment and monitoring integrations for the developer workflow). Stripe provides direct integrations with over 40 payment methods globally and works with fraud prevention partners (Signifyd, Riskified, Forter). The Stripe REST API is comprehensive, supporting all platform operations including charges, customers, subscriptions, invoices, disputes, refunds, reports, and Connect platform management. The API supports idempotency keys for safe retries, webhook signatures for event verification, and granular API key permissions that restrict access to specific resources (read-only keys, keys limited to specific products).

content.title: Security & Compliance
content.body: Stripe is PCI DSS Level 1 certified (the highest level of payment card industry compliance), SOC 2 Type II and SOC 3 certified, ISO 27001 certified, and GDPR compliant. All card data is tokenized — Stripe returns a token representing the card rather than the full card number, so merchants never handle or store raw PAN data. Data in transit is encrypted with TLS 1.2+ and data at rest is encrypted with AES-256. Stripe supports 3D Secure 2 authentication for liability shift on authenticated transactions, reducing merchant chargeback liability to zero for authenticated transactions. Radar fraud detection evaluates every transaction in real-time. API security features include granular key permissions (read-only, restricted to specific products, IP-restricted), two-factor authentication for dashboard access, and webhook signature verification with rotating signing secrets. Stripe is compliant with PSD2/SCA in the European Economic Area, supports data residency in US, EU, and Canada regions, and provides annual penetration test results upon request with an NDA. Stripe maintains a public vulnerability disclosure program with bounties up to $10,000. For businesses requiring dedicated compliance support, Stripe provides PCI compliance questionnaires and attestation of compliance for SAQ A or SAQ A-EP merchants.

content.title: Performance
content.body: Stripe's payment API responds in 200-400ms at the 95th percentile for standard card charges processed in US data centers, with international transactions adding 100-300ms depending on the card network routing. The API is designed for at-least-once delivery semantics with idempotency — retries are safe and guaranteed not to create duplicate charges. Stripe's infrastructure handles millions of concurrent requests with documented 99.99% uptime for core payment processing endpoints, though the Stripe Dashboard and Sigma analytics experience occasional slower response during peak US business hours (2-5 second page loads). Webhook delivery latency averages 1-3 seconds from event occurrence to endpoint delivery, with exponential backoff retries for failed deliveries over 3 days. Stripe has experienced three significant outages in 2025: a 45-minute payment processing interruption in March, a 90-minute Connect API degradation in July, and a 30-minute Dashboard access issue in November. The Stripe API returns 503 status codes during maintenance windows (typically Sunday 2-4 AM Pacific) with 15-minute advance notice via the status page. Stripe publishes real-time status at status.stripe.com with component-level granularity and post-incident reports within 7 days of any major outage.

content.title: Customer Support
content.body: Stripe support tiers are determined by processing volume. Standard accounts receive email-only support through the dashboard with 24-48 hour response time for non-critical issues. Accounts processing over $1 million annually receive priority email support with 12-hour response time. Accounts over $10 million annually receive a dedicated account manager, phone support during business hours, and quarterly business reviews. Stripe Enterprise ($100M+ annual processing) includes 24/7 phone support with 1-hour critical severity response, dedicated solutions engineer, and custom SLA. Support quality is the lowest-rated aspect of Stripe across review platforms (3.8/5), with consistent complaints about response delays during time-sensitive chargeback disputes, lack of phone support for mid-market accounts, and templated responses that do not address specific business context. Stripe's documentation and developer resources are exceptional: comprehensive API reference, step-by-step integration guides for every product, 100+ code examples in multiple languages, a community forum with 50,000+ threads moderated by Stripe engineers, in-person workshops (Stripe Sessions conference, local meetups), and Stripe Press publishing technical books on payments and financial systems. The knowledge base covers 500+ topics, and the Stripe Community forum typically provides answers within 2-4 hours from other developers and Stripe staff.

content.title: Real-world Use Cases
content.body: A B2B SaaS company processing $2 million annually in subscription revenue uses Stripe Billing with annual and monthly plan options, automated dunning that sends payment reminder emails at days 3, 7, 14, and 21 after failed payment, and Smart Retries that have recovered 38% of failed recurring payments by retrying at times optimized for each card network (Visa retries perform best on weekday mornings, Amex on weekday afternoons). A global e-commerce marketplace operating across 15 countries uses Stripe Connect to onboard 5,000+ sellers with automated KYC verification that checks identity documents against government databases, splits each transaction 85/15 between seller and marketplace, holds funds in escrow until delivery confirmation, and facilitates instant payouts to sellers through Stripe Treasury debit cards — replacing a manual payout process that required 3 full-time finance staff. A subscription box business uses Stripe Tax configured with product tax codes mapping each box type to the correct US state sales tax treatment (snack boxes taxed as food in most states, cosmetic boxes taxed as tangible personal property), automatically collecting the correct tax rate for each customer's shipping address across 12,000+ US tax jurisdictions and generating jurisdiction-level tax reports for monthly filing.

content.title: Common Mistakes
content.body: Five mistakes repeatedly emerge from Stripe integration audits. Testing with the same card numbers throughout development — using only the default 4242 test card means the team never validates handling of edge cases like 3D Secure authentication (requires specific test card numbers from Stripe's test card documentation), card declines for insufficient funds (4000000000009995), or expired cards (4000000000000069). Always test with every test card number that matches your payment method scenarios. Ignoring idempotency is the second most common error: without idempotency keys on API requests, network retries during timeouts can create duplicate charges that require manual refunds and customer communication. Third, mishandling webhook idempotency: Stripe delivers webhooks at least once, and identical events may be sent multiple times during infrastructure issues. Every webhook handler must check for duplicate event processing using the event ID. Fourth, using live mode API keys in client-side code — secret keys exposed in browser-side JavaScript or mobile app source code allow anyone to read and modify your Stripe account. Test keys start with sk_test_, live keys start with sk_live_, and production environments should use restricted keys whenever possible. Fifth, underestimating dispute management: businesses that ignore the dispute process or fail to respond within Stripe's 7-day evidence submission window lose 100% of disputed amounts plus the $15 fee.

content.title: Tips from experienced users
content.body: Experienced Stripe integrators rely on several patterns. Use Stripe metadata fields extensively — every Stripe object (customers, subscriptions, invoices, payment intents) accepts up to 50 key-value metadata pairs of 500 characters each. Store internal identifiers (user IDs, order IDs, SKU codes, salesperson IDs) as metadata on every object so that reconciliation queries and custom reports can join Stripe data with internal systems without external mapping tables. Set up webhook endpoint monitoring with dead-letter tracking: configure a secondary endpoint that receives all failed webhook deliveries after the 3-day retry window, and set up an alert (PagerDuty, Slack) so that missed events (failed subscription payments, successful chargebacks) are surfaced within 24 hours rather than discovered during the next finance review. Use Stripe's Tax product even if you have a small number of customers — tax rates change frequently (especially US sales tax rates which update quarterly from 2,000+ jurisdictions), and manual rate tracking inevitably misses changes that result in audit exposure. Use restricted API keys for every integration: create separate keys for your production server, staging server, mobile app, and each third-party integration (Shopify, WooCommerce, Zapier).

content.title: Alternatives
content.body: Stripe's primary competitors serve different payment processing priorities and business models. Adyen offers interchange-plus pricing (a markup over actual card network interchange fees) with lower effective rates for high-volume processing at $5M+/year, with stronger in-person payment support through POS terminals and unified commerce reporting. PayPal provides simpler setup for non-technical businesses with 2.59% + $0.49 standard rates, stronger buyer protection and brand recognition that actually increases conversion on some e-commerce segments, but weaker developer tools and subscription management. Square dominates in-person payments with integrated hardware, instant card reader pairing, and vertical-specific POS software (retail, restaurants, appointments) at 2.6% + $0.10 per transaction, but its online APIs are less mature than Stripe's. Braintree (PayPal) offers similar developer-friendly APIs with PayPal payment method inclusion at 2.59% + $0.49, and the key differentiator of PayPal's Venmo integration for US consumers. Braintree's documentation is considered less polished than Stripe's, and new feature availability typically lags 6-12 months behind Stripe. For businesses solely in the US or a single EU market, local processors (Square in US, Mollie in Netherlands, GoCardless for direct debit) can offer simpler integration and lower rates, at the cost of limited multi-currency and cross-border capabilities.

content.title: Final Verdict
content.body: Stripe earns a 4.7/5 rating as the best payment infrastructure platform for internet businesses, with unmatched developer experience, the broadest payment method support, and a financial services platform that extends well beyond basic payment processing into tax, banking, identity, and revenue automation. It is not the cheapest option (high-volume businesses will pay less with interchange-plus processors like Adyen), not the best for high-risk industries (specialized processors are more stable), and not the most accessible for non-technical founders (Square and PayPal offer simpler paths to first payment). For SaaS companies, subscription businesses, e-commerce merchants with international customers, and marketplace platforms, Stripe provides the fastest path to a production payments system with ongoing cost that is competitive for businesses processing under $5 million annually. The platform's support quality is the primary concern — businesses processing over $10 million annually should negotiate Enterprise terms with dedicated support to avoid the frustration of email-only support during payment disputes. Buy Stripe for the API experience and product breadth; negotiate pricing at $1 million annual volume; invest in test mode integration testing with all relevant test card numbers before processing your first live transaction.

faqs.question: What is Stripe best used for?
faqs.answer: Stripe is best used as the payment processing infrastructure for internet businesses that need to accept payments online, manage subscriptions, handle marketplace payouts, or operate internationally. Its API-first design makes it the standard choice for SaaS companies, e-commerce platforms, and subscription businesses that want a developer-friendly payment system with global reach.

faqs.question: How much does Stripe cost?
faqs.answer: Stripe charges 2.9% + $0.30 per successful card charge for standard payments. International cards cost an additional 1.5%. ACH direct debit is 0.8% capped at $5. Instant payouts cost 1% ($0.50 minimum). Stripe Billing costs 0.5% of recurring revenue. Radar for Fraud Prevention costs $0.02 per transaction for the standard tier. There are no monthly fees, setup fees, or minimum processing requirements. Volume discounts require negotiation above $1 million annually.

faqs.question: Does Stripe integrate with other tools?
faqs.answer: Stripe integrates with major e-commerce platforms (Shopify, WooCommerce, BigCommerce), accounting software (QuickBooks, Xero, NetSuite, FreshBooks), CRM tools (Salesforce, HubSpot), analytics platforms (Mixpanel, Amplitude), and development tools (GitHub, Vercel, Netlify). The Stripe Apps marketplace offers 200+ third-party plugins, and the REST API enables custom integrations with any platform.

faqs.question: Is Stripe secure?
faqs.answer: Stripe is PCI DSS Level 1 certified, SOC 2 Type II compliant, and GDPR compliant. All card data is tokenized — Stripe never returns full card numbers to your servers. Radar fraud detection evaluates every transaction in real-time. Stripe supports 3D Secure 2 authentication for liability shift on disputed transactions. API keys use granular permissions and can be restricted by IP address. Stripe publishes annual penetration test results and maintains a vulnerability disclosure program.
